_meta.description: Hand-curated registry of Pāli terms whose translation is genuinely contested across schools/translators. Each entry has parses with primary citations (no LLM-claimed sources). Per docs/sutta-studio/GROUNDING.md Phase 1.
_meta.verification: Every URL in this file has been fetched via curl and returned HTTP 200 within 24h of the lastUpdated date. Translator renderings are quoted from sources accessible via the URLs; Wikipedia citations are quoted from article text fetched on 2026-05-14.
antiPatterns: Don't add an entry whose citations are LLM-claimed rather than URL-verified.
Don't claim a translator renders a term as X without a clickable source.
When the scholarship genuinely disagrees, surface BOTH positions — don't pick a winner.

parses.summary: Reads paṭṭhāna as a derivative noun from paṭi-sthā ('to stand against, to set forth'). Yields 'foundation of mindfulness' — the historical translation convention through the 20th century.

translatorRenderings.context: MLDB (Middle Length Discourses of the Buddha) — the canonical English MN10

parses.summary: Reads paṭṭhāna as upaṭṭhāna with consonant assimilation (upa-tt-hāna). Yields 'establishment of mindfulness' or 'presence of mindfulness'. Defended philologically by Anālayo on the basis that 'paṭṭhāna' appears only in Abhidhamma texts whereas 'upaṭṭhāna' is readily visible in the Nikayas and Sanskrit parallels.

translatorRenderings.context: SuttaCentral MN10 — Sujato's recent translation
dhammatalks.org MN10 — Thanissaro evolved from his earlier 'frames of reference' rendering to 'establishings'

encyclopedicReferences.quotedClaim: Bhikkhu Anālayo, Bhikkhu Bodhi, and Thanissaro Bhikkhu favor the upaṭṭhāna parsing; traditional Pāli commentary scholars favor the paṭṭhāna parsing. Anālayo argues 'paṭṭhāna' appears only in Abhidhamma texts whereas 'upaṭṭhāna' is readily visible in the Nikayas.

primaryScholarship.ref: Anālayo, Bhikkhu. 'Satipaṭṭhāna: The Direct Path to Realization' (Windhorse Publications, 2003)
primaryScholarship.note: Monograph-length defense of the upaṭṭhāna parse. Chapter 1 contains the philological argument.

satipaṭṭhāna.curatorNote: Critical care needed: the parse choice (paṭṭhāna vs upaṭṭhāna) and the translation choice ('foundations' vs 'establishments') are separable decisions. Bodhi's MLDB keeps 'foundations' as a translation choice for continuity with prior English literature, even though Bodhi's philological position aligns with Anālayo on upaṭṭhāna. Don't conflate the two.

parses.gloss: mechanical metaphor — a wheel whose axle-hole is poorly bored, hence wobble and friction
parses.summary: The 'axle-hole' etymology, pedagogically vivid. The metaphor is mechanical (wobble in the wheel of experience) rather than metaphysical.

encyclopedicReferences.quotedClaim: The word kha, in later Sanskrit meaning 'sky,' 'ether,' or 'space,' was originally the word for 'hole,' particularly an axle hole, with du- meaning 'bad,' resulting in a bumpy ride analogy.

parses.summary: Less commonly cited but philologically defensible. Foregrounds the felt-quality of dis-ease rather than the mechanical metaphor.

encyclopedicReferences.quotedClaim: dukkha as derived from duḥ-sthā, 'standing badly', conveys nuances of 'uneasiness' or of being 'uncomfortable'.

translatorRenderings.rendering: unsatisfactoriness (for the bare term in explanatory contexts); 'suffering' for the bare translation
translatorRenderings.context: Bodhi distinguishes the technical-philosophical 'unsatisfactoriness' from the conventional 'suffering' depending on register.
Particularly in Dhammacakkappavattana Sutta and related foundational texts.

encyclopedicReferences.quotedClaim: Thanissaro Bhikkhu uses 'stress' in translations of the Dhammacakkappavattana Sutta.

dukkha.curatorNote: The 'axle-hole' etymology is the more commonly cited one and is pedagogically powerful — it grounds the abstract concept in a concrete physical metaphor. But the duḥ-sthā derivation is also defensible and shifts the felt-quality emphasis. Modern scholarship (Monier-Williams, Harvey, Anālayo) doesn't fully settle the question.

parses.gloss: blowing out, extinguishment — the cooling of a fire that has gone out
parses.summary: The classical etymology. The compound's literal sense is 'extinguished' (passive past participle), referring to the cooling of the three fires (greed, hatred, delusion).

encyclopedicReferences.quotedClaim: Collins says the word nirvāṇa is from the verbal root vā 'blow' in the form of past participle vāna 'blown', prefixed with the preverb nis meaning 'out'.

doctrinalContext.name: The three fires reading
doctrinalContext.summary: Nibbāna as extinguishing of the three unwholesome roots (rāga, dosa, moha). Without this context, 'extinguishment' misreads as nihilistic annihilation.

encyclopedicReferences.quotedClaim: The Buddhist texts identify these 'three fires' or 'three poisons' as raga (greed, sensuality), dvesha (aversion, hate) and avidyā or moha (ignorance, delusion).

translatorRenderings.context: MN10 translation. Thanissaro's signature rendering; emphasises freedom-from-the-aggregates over the cooling-of-fires metaphor.
MLDB MN10. Bodhi preserves nibbāna as a technical term to avoid imported baggage from English equivalents.
SuttaCentral MN10. Sujato recovers the literal blowing-out metaphor; this aligns with his consistent rendering of the three fires as 'greed, hate, delusion'.

nibbāna.curatorNote: Bodhi's untranslated choice and Sujato's 'extinguishment' both reflect philological care; Thanissaro's 'unbinding' is a theological emphasis on freedom-from. All three are defensible. The reader sees the disagreement empirically by following the citation chips.

parses.gloss: the principle by which one knows; the systematic-procedural way
parses.summary: Action-noun from the cognate of Sanskrit nyāya (logical system, method). In Pāli Buddhism the rational-procedural sense is anchored to soteriology — not abstract logic but the logic of liberation.

translatorRenderings.context: MLDB MN10
SuttaCentral MN10
dhammatalks.org MN10

parses.morphology: ñāya as commentarial gloss for ariyaṭṭhaṅgika-magga
parses.summary: Buddhaghosa's Visuddhimagga and the Aṭṭhakathā tradition gloss ñāya directly as the Noble Eightfold Path. The strongest doctrinal reading: 'attainment of ñāya' = 'arriving at the Eightfold Path'.
parses.needsVerification: Visuddhimagga citation pending — Phase 4 commentarial-gloss seed will provide the line-level VRI URL.

ñāya.curatorNote: The commentarial reading (Buddhaghosa's gloss) is the strongest doctrinal reading and is what makes this phrase the structural pivot of the MN10 formula chain. The three contemporary translator schools each shade the procedural-technical sense slightly differently.

parses.summary: Sati keeps the memory-resonance: mindfulness as the faculty of REMEMBERING what is happening, KEEPING IN MIND. The English 'mindfulness' was Rhys Davids' coinage to translate sati while preserving the memory-emphasis.

translatorRenderings.context: MLDB — the standard English convention
SuttaCentral MN10
dhammatalks.org MN10

doctrinalContext.name: Theravāda gloss as apilāpana (un-floating)
doctrinalContext.summary: Theravāda commentaries gloss sati as apilāpana — un-floating, keeping things from drifting away. The cognitive function is anti-distraction: holding the object of meditation in the field of awareness so it doesn't slip out.
doctrinalContext.needsVerification: Visuddhimagga citation pending — Phase 4 commentarial-gloss seed will provide the line-level VRI URL.

sati.curatorNote: Translation is uncontested ('mindfulness' is the universal rendering since Rhys Davids), but the meaning of sati within practice has produced a large secondary literature (Anālayo, Wallace, etc.) about how the cognitive function relates to memory, attention, and ethical sensitivity. The contested space is interpretive, not lexical.

parses.morphology: from ātāpa (heat, ardor) — possessive adjective, 'one who has ātāpa'
parses.summary: Adjective describing the meditator's quality of effort. The Pāli ātāpa is literal 'heat' — the metaphor is meditation-as-burning-effort, the contemplative who applies fire to the work.

translatorRenderings.context: MLDB MN10 — Bodhi's standard rendering across the satipaṭṭhāna formula
SuttaCentral MN10 (bilara-verified) — Sujato's 'keen, aware, and mindful' formula
dhammatalks.org MN10 — Thanissaro's 'ardent, alert, & mindful' formula. Same rendering as Bodhi here.

encyclopedicReferences.quotedClaim: Wikipedia article on ātāpī as the quality of effort in satipaṭṭhāna practice.

ātāpī.curatorNote: Bodhi and Thanissaro converge on 'ardent' (preserving the fire-metaphor); Sujato chose 'keen' for register accessibility. The disagreement is register-only — all three preserve the energetic-effort sense.

parses.morphology: saṁ- (together, fully) + pajānāti (knows) → possessive 'one who fully knows'
parses.summary: Adjective. Saṁ- intensifier on the verb of knowing — the meditator who has the cognitive quality of full, clear comprehension of what is occurring.

translatorRenderings.context: MLDB MN10 — Bodhi's standard rendering, preserves the precision-of-knowing emphasis
SuttaCentral MN10 (bilara-verified) — Sujato's 'keen, aware, and mindful' formula
dhammatalks.org MN10 — Thanissaro's 'ardent, alert, & mindful' formula

encyclopedicReferences.quotedClaim: Wikipedia article on sampajañña (the abstract noun form) — the quality of clear comprehension in mindfulness practice.

sampajāno.curatorNote: Three traditions diverge sharply: Bodhi preserves the technical 'clearly comprehending' (closest to literal); Sujato simplifies to 'aware' (register-accessibility); Thanissaro picks 'alert' (cognitive-readiness emphasis). All three are defensible; the rendering choice shifts the felt emphasis from precision-of-knowing → simple awareness → readiness-to-respond.

parses.summary: The technical term for the felt quality of experience — pleasant, painful, or neutral. NOT 'emotion' in the modern psychological sense. Vedanā is the bare hedonic tone of any sense-contact, before cognitive elaboration.

translatorRenderings.context: MLDB — Bodhi's standard. Preserves Western philosophical tradition's 'feeling' but flag the technical narrowness in his footnotes.
SuttaCentral MN10 (bilara-verified) — Sujato also uses 'feeling'. 'observing an aspect of feelings' for vedanānupassī.
dhammatalks.org MN10 — 'remains focused on feelings in & of themselves' for vedanānupassī.

encyclopedicReferences.quotedClaim: Wikipedia article on vedanā as the second of the five aggregates; the three feeling-tones (pleasant, painful, neutral).

vedanā.curatorNote: Translation is uncontested ('feeling' or 'feelings'). The interpretive contestation is about what vedanā DOES in meditation — is it the bare hedonic tone, or does it include reactive feelings (Goenka tradition)? Bodhi and Anālayo argue for the bare-tone reading; some meditation lineages collapse the distinction. Lexically uncontested, doctrinally contested.

parses.morphology: from √cit (to think, to attend) — noun for that-which-thinks
parses.gloss: mind, heart, thought-process; the cognizing dimension of experience
parses.summary: One of the three near-synonyms in Pāli psychology (citta, mano, viññāṇa). In satipaṭṭhāna context, citta refers to the meditator's mind-as-object — the field of cognition being observed, distinct from the contents observed in it.

translatorRenderings.context: MLDB MN10. Standard rendering — preserves the cognitive-faculty sense.
SuttaCentral MN10 (bilara-verified) — 'observing an aspect of the mind' for cittānupassī.
dhammatalks.org MN10 — 'remains focused on the mind in & of itself' for cittānupassī.

encyclopedicReferences.quotedClaim: Wikipedia article distinguishing citta from mano and viññāṇa in Pāli psychology.

citta.curatorNote: Translation is uncontested ('mind' universally). The interpretive question is which Pāli term renders best as 'mind' — citta or mano or viññāṇa. Anālayo's monograph argues citta is the appropriate object of mindfulness (the cognitive faculty as field), not viññāṇa (the discriminating consciousness as process).

parses.morphology: plural of dhamma — from √dhṛ (to hold, to support)
parses.summary: The MOST polysemous word in the Pāli canon. In satipaṭṭhāna context (dhammānupassī), refers to mental-qualities to be observed — wholesome/unwholesome states, hindrances, awakening factors. NOT 'the Dhamma' (the Buddha's teaching). The plural marks the specific 'objects' sense.

translatorRenderings.context: MLDB MN10. Bodhi's earlier choice; later editions sometimes shift to 'phenomena'.
SuttaCentral MN10 (bilara-verified) — 'observing an aspect of principles' for dhammānupassī.
dhammatalks.org MN10 — 'remains focused on mental qualities in & of themselves' for dhammānupassī.

encyclopedicReferences.quotedClaim: Wikipedia article on the multiple senses of dhamma/dharma — teaching, phenomenon, mental quality, ultimate law.

dhammā.curatorNote: Three traditions on dhammā in MN10's contemplation context: Bodhi 'mind-objects' (cognitive-objects sense), Sujato 'principles' (abstract regularity sense), Thanissaro 'mental qualities' (psychological-states sense). Each picks a different facet of dhamma's polysemy. This is THE most contested compound word in MN10's body — readers familiar with one tradition often misread the others.

parses.gloss: one who contemplates the body; participle form of the contemplation
parses.summary: Compound participle. The anu- prefix is iterative/directional — continuous, sustained observation rather than a single look. The four anupassī compounds in MN10 (kāyānupassī, vedanānupassī, cittānupassī, dhammānupassī) name the four foundations of mindfulness in action.

translatorRenderings.rendering: contemplating the body in the body
translatorRenderings.context: MLDB MN10 — Bodhi preserves the doubled-noun construction (kāye kāyānupassī = 'contemplating body in body')

translatorRenderings.rendering: meditates by observing an aspect of the body
translatorRenderings.context: SuttaCentral MN10 (bilara-verified) — Sujato unpacks the kāye + kāyānupassī construction as 'aspect of body' for clarity

translatorRenderings.rendering: remains focused on the body in & of itself
translatorRenderings.context: dhammatalks.org MN10 — Thanissaro renders the locative construction as 'in & of itself' to emphasise the non-dualistic framing

kāyānupassī.curatorNote: The locative-doubling construction (kāye kāyānupassī = 'in body, contemplating body') is itself contested. Bodhi preserves it literally; Sujato unpacks to 'aspect of body'; Thanissaro renders as 'body in & of itself' (the noumenal-emphasis reading). The four anupassī formulas all share this structure — same disagreement pattern applies to vedanā, citta, dhammā.
